1:
"Paul, a servant of God, and an apostle of Jesus Christ, according to the faith of God's elect, and the acknowledging of the truth which is after godliness;" The witnesses note the apostolic salutation and the aim of Paul's office. Chrysostom notes Titus was an approved companion of Paul; otherwise Paul would not have committed to him the charge of the whole island of Crete, nor commanded him to set in order the things that were wanting. The witnesses note Paul opening as a servant of God and apostle of Jesus Christ, according to the faith of God's elect and the acknowledging of the truth which is after godliness; the apostleship aimed at the elect's faith and a truth that breeds godliness — that Paul names himself "a servant of God" (bound to his service) and "an apostle of Jesus Christ" (sent by his authority), and states the end of his office: it is "according to the faith of God's elect" (to bring the chosen to faith) "and the acknowledging of the truth which is after godliness" — the truth that is no barren notion but accords with and produces godliness — so that the very salutation marks the gospel as a truth that always tends to holy living; the faith of God's elect, and the acknowledging of the truth which is after godliness. The witnesses note Paul, a servant of God, and an apostle of Jesus Christ. The witnesses note the acknowledging of the truth which is after godliness.

The witnesses note Paul's apostleship aimed at the faith of God's elect and a truth "after godliness" — the gospel no barren notion but a truth that produces holy living.

So the witnesses make the verse the apostleship aimed at faith and godliness. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul's office serves the elect's faith and a truth that always tends to holiness. The witnesses note the servant: "Paul, a servant of God" — bound to God's service; the servant; the of God; the bound; the service; the slave. The expositors observe the apostle: "and an apostle of Jesus Christ" — sent by Christ's authority; the apostle; the of Christ; the sent; the authority; the office. The witnesses press the faith of the elect: "according to the faith of God's elect" — to bring the chosen to faith; the faith of the elect; the the chosen; the to faith; the the end; the the elect. The expositors draw the lesson on the truth after godliness: "the acknowledging of the truth which is after godliness" — a truth that accords with and produces godliness; the truth after godliness; the produces; the accords; the holiness; the no barren notion. The witnesses observe Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes Titus an approved companion, trusted with all Crete; the approved; the companion; the all Crete; the trusted; the the charge. The expositors note the holy living: the gospel always tends to holy living; the holy living; the the gospel; the tends; the godliness; the produces. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the a servant of God and an apostle of Jesus Christ, the the acknowledging of the truth which is after godliness, and the aim of the office. The verse thus records the apostleship aimed at faith and godliness — teaching that Paul's office serves the elect's faith and a truth that tends to holiness.
2:
"In hope of eternal life, which God, that cannot lie, promised before the world began;" The witnesses note the hope of eternal life promised by the God who cannot lie. Gill notes this is another end of the gospel ministry — to bring God's elect to the hope of eternal glory and happiness; and this hope rests on the promise of God, who cannot lie. The witnesses note Paul declaring that this faith and truth are in hope of eternal life, which God, who cannot lie, promised before the world began; the sure hope grounded in the eternal promise of the truthful God — that Paul names the great object toward which his ministry leads: "the hope of eternal life," and gives the firmest possible ground for that hope — it rests on what "God, that cannot lie" (the God whose very nature forbids falsehood) "promised before the world began" (in the eternal counsel and covenant, from everlasting) — so that the believer's hope of glory stands not on a changing thing but on the unbreakable word of the God who cannot lie, given before time itself; which God, that cannot lie, promised before the world began. The witnesses note in hope of eternal life. The witnesses note which God, that cannot lie, promised before the world began.

The witnesses note the hope of eternal life — resting on the promise of the God who cannot lie, given before the world began; a hope grounded on his unbreakable word.

So the witnesses make the verse the sure hope grounded in God's eternal promise. The lesson the expositors draw is that the hope of eternal life rests on the unbreakable promise of the truthful God, given from everlasting. The witnesses note the hope: "in hope of eternal life" — Gill notes the gospel brings the elect to the hope of glory; the hope; the eternal life; the glory; the the object; the the end. The expositors observe the God cannot lie: "which God, that cannot lie" — the God whose nature forbids falsehood; the God cannot lie; the cannot lie; the truthful; the his nature; the no falsehood. The witnesses press the promised: "promised" — the hope rests on God's promise; the promised; the the promise; the rests; the the ground; the sure. The expositors draw the lesson on the before the world began: "before the world began" — in the eternal counsel, from everlasting; the before the world; the eternal counsel; the from everlasting; the the covenant; the before time. The witnesses observe the unbreakable word: the hope stands on the unbreakable word of God; the unbreakable word; the stands; the firm; the sure; the the word. The expositors note the firmest ground: this is the firmest possible ground for hope; the firmest ground; the the ground; the for hope; the sure; the firm. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the in hope of eternal life, the which God that cannot lie promised before the world began, and the sure hope. The verse thus records the sure hope grounded in God's eternal promise — teaching that the hope of eternal life rests on the unbreakable promise of the truthful God.
3:
"But hath in due times manifested his word through preaching, which is committed unto me according to the commandment of God our Saviour;" The witnesses note the manifesting of God's word in due time through preaching committed to Paul. Barnes notes "in due times" means at the proper time, the time God had intended, the best time. The witnesses note Paul declaring that God has in due times manifested his word through preaching, which was committed to him by the commandment of God our Saviour; the eternal promise now openly published through the preaching entrusted to Paul — that Paul shows how the ancient promise (v.2) has come to light: God "hath in due times manifested his word" — what was hidden in his eternal counsel he has, at the appointed and best season, made openly known — and that "through preaching" (the public proclamation of the gospel), which proclamation "is committed unto me" as a sacred trust "according to the commandment of God our Saviour"; so that the everlasting purpose is now made manifest by the preached word, the very office laid on Paul by God's own command; manifested his word through preaching, which is committed unto me. The witnesses note hath in due times manifested his word through preaching. The witnesses note which is committed unto me according to the commandment of God our Saviour.

The witnesses note God's eternal word now manifested in due time through preaching — the ancient promise openly published by the proclamation entrusted to Paul.

So the witnesses make the verse the eternal promise now published through preaching. The lesson the expositors draw is that the ancient promise is now made manifest by the preached word committed to Paul by God's command. The witnesses note the manifested: "hath in due times manifested his word" — what was hidden God has made known; the manifested; the his word; the made known; the hidden; the revealed. The expositors observe the due times: Barnes notes "in due times" — at the proper, best, appointed time; the due times; the proper time; the best; the appointed; the season. The witnesses press the through preaching: "through preaching" — through the public proclamation of the gospel; the through preaching; the proclamation; the the gospel; the public; the preached. The expositors draw the lesson on the committed: "which is committed unto me" — committed to Paul as a sacred trust; the committed; the to Paul; the sacred trust; the entrusted; the the office. The witnesses observe the commandment: "according to the commandment of God our Saviour" — by God's own command; the commandment; the of God; the our Saviour; the command; the laid on. The expositors note the everlasting purpose: the everlasting purpose is now made manifest by the word; the everlasting purpose; the now manifest; the the word; the published; the revealed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the hath in due times manifested his word through preaching, the which is committed unto me according to the commandment of God our Saviour, and the promise now published. The verse thus records the eternal promise now published through preaching — teaching that the ancient promise is made manifest by the preached word committed to Paul.
4:
"To Titus, mine own son after the common faith: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ our Saviour." The witnesses note the address to Titus and the benediction. Gill notes Titus was Paul's own son not in a natural but a spiritual sense, the apostle being the instrument of his conversion, and so his spiritual father; and "after the common faith," the faith common to all the elect. The witnesses note Paul addressing Titus as his own son after the common faith, with grace, mercy, and peace from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ; the affectionate address and full blessing to a fellow-worker — that Paul greets Titus as "mine own son" — a true spiritual son, converted under his ministry — and "after the common faith," that is, in the one faith shared by all believers (Jew and Gentile alike, who are equally the elect of God); and he invokes the threefold blessing of "grace, mercy, and peace" from the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ "our Saviour," the fuller benediction (with "mercy") fitting for a minister, all flowing from the Father and the Son together; to Titus, mine own son after the common faith. The witnesses note to Titus, mine own son after the common faith. The witnesses note grace, mercy, and peace, from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ our Saviour.

The witnesses note Paul greeting Titus as his own son after the common faith, with grace, mercy, and peace — the affectionate address and full blessing to a fellow-worker.

So the witnesses make the verse the address and benediction to Titus. The lesson the expositors draw is the affectionate spiritual sonship and the full minister's blessing from Father and Son. The witnesses note the own son: "to Titus, mine own son" — Gill notes Titus converted under Paul's ministry, his spiritual son; the own son; the spiritual; the converted; the his father; the the son. The expositors observe the common faith: "after the common faith" — in the one faith shared by all believers; the common faith; the shared; the all believers; the the one faith; the the elect. The witnesses press the grace mercy peace: "Grace, mercy, and peace" — the threefold blessing; the grace mercy peace; the threefold; the blessing; the the benediction; the full. The expositors draw the lesson on the mercy: "mercy" added, fitting for a minister; the mercy; the added; the the minister; the fuller; the fitting. The witnesses observe the from Father and Son: "from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ our Saviour" — flowing from the Father and the Son together; the from Father and Son; the the Father; the the Son; the our Saviour; the together. The expositors note the fellow-worker: the affectionate address to a fellow-worker; the fellow-worker; the affectionate; the Titus; the the address; the son. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the to Titus mine own son after the common faith, the grace mercy and peace from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ our Saviour, and the address and benediction. The verse thus records the address and benediction to Titus — teaching the affectionate spiritual sonship and the full minister's blessing from Father and Son.
5:
"For this cause left I thee in Crete, that thou shouldest set in order the things that are wanting, and ordain elders in every city, as I had appointed thee:" The witnesses note the reason Titus was left in Crete — to set the churches in order. Chrysostom notes the whole life of the ancients was one of action and contention, while ours is one of indolence; they knew they were brought into the world to labour according to God's will. The witnesses note Paul declaring that he left Titus in Crete to set in order the things wanting and ordain elders in every city; the commission to complete and organize the Cretan churches — that Paul recalls the purpose for which he had left Titus on the island: not to rest, but with a working charge "that thou shouldest set in order the things that are wanting" (to put right and complete what was yet lacking in the newly-planted churches) "and ordain elders in every city" (appoint qualified pastors and overseers in each place), "as I had appointed thee" — so that Titus's task was the great work of organizing, completing, and providing settled leadership for the churches; set in order the things that are wanting, and ordain elders in every city. The witnesses note that thou shouldest set in order the things that are wanting. The witnesses note and ordain elders in every city, as I had appointed thee.

The witnesses note Titus left in Crete to set in order what was wanting and ordain elders in every city — the great work of completing and organizing the churches.

So the witnesses make the verse the commission to organize the churches. The lesson the expositors draw is that Titus was left to complete the unfinished churches and appoint qualified elders in each city. The witnesses note the left in Crete: "for this cause left I thee in Crete" — Paul left Titus with a working charge; the left in Crete; the the charge; the the purpose; the not rest; the the work. The expositors observe the set in order: "that thou shouldest set in order the things that are wanting" — to complete what was yet lacking; the set in order; the the things wanting; the complete; the put right; the lacking. The witnesses press the ordain elders: "and ordain elders in every city" — appoint qualified pastors in each place; the ordain elders; the appoint; the pastors; the every city; the overseers. The expositors draw the lesson on the as appointed: "as I had appointed thee" — as Paul had directed him; the as appointed; the directed; the the charge; the appointed; the ordered. The witnesses observe the organizing: Titus's task was organizing and completing the churches; the organizing; the completing; the the churches; the the work; the settled. The expositors note Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes the ancients lived for labour, not indolence; the the ancients; the labour; the not indolence; the God's will; the work. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the set in order the things that are wanting, the ordain elders in every city as I had appointed thee, and the commission to organize. The verse thus records the commission to organize the churches — teaching that Titus was left to complete the unfinished churches and appoint qualified elders.
6:
"If any be blameless, the husband of one wife, having faithful children not accused of riot or unruly." The witnesses note the qualifications of an elder in his life and household. Gill notes he must be blameless in his outward life and conversation, not chargeable with any notorious crime. Matthew Henry notes the apostle gives directions about ordination, pointing out the qualifications and virtues of those to be ordained. The witnesses note Paul setting the elder's qualifications — blameless, the husband of one wife, having faithful, well-ordered children; the proven character and ordered home required of an elder — that Paul, beginning the list of qualifications for those Titus is to ordain, requires first that the man be "blameless" (of unblemished reputation, against whom no just charge can be brought), "the husband of one wife" (faithful and chaste in marriage, not a polygamist or of loose life), and well-ordered in his own home, "having faithful children not accused of riot or unruly" (children who are believing, and orderly rather than wild or rebellious) — so that the elder must be proved in his own character and household before he is set over God's house; the husband of one wife, having faithful children. The witnesses note if any be blameless, the husband of one wife. The witnesses note having faithful children not accused of riot or unruly.

The witnesses note the elder's qualifications — blameless, the husband of one wife, with faithful, well-ordered children; proven in character and home before ruling God's house.

So the witnesses make the verse the proven character and home of an elder. The lesson the expositors draw is that the elder must be blameless, faithful in marriage, and well-ordered in his household. The witnesses note the blameless: "if any be blameless" — Gill notes of unblemished reputation, no notorious crime; the blameless; the unblemished; the reputation; the no charge; the proved. The expositors observe the husband of one wife: "the husband of one wife" — faithful and chaste in marriage; the husband of one wife; the faithful; the chaste; the marriage; the not loose. The witnesses press the faithful children: "having faithful children" — children who are believing and well-ordered; the faithful children; the believing; the well-ordered; the the home; the orderly. The expositors draw the lesson on the not riot: "not accused of riot or unruly" — not wild or rebellious; the not riot; the not unruly; the not wild; the not rebellious; the orderly. The witnesses observe the ordered home: the elder must be well-ordered in his own home; the ordered home; the his household; the well-ordered; the the home; the ruled. The expositors note Matthew Henry's note: Henry notes Paul gives the qualifications for ordination; the the qualifications; the ordination; the virtues; the the list; the directed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the if any be blameless the husband of one wife, the having faithful children not accused of riot or unruly, and the proven character. The verse thus records the proven character and home of an elder — teaching that the elder must be blameless, faithful in marriage, and well-ordered in his household.
7:
"For a bishop must be blameless, as the steward of God; not selfwilled, not soon angry, not given to wine, no striker, not given to filthy lucre;" The witnesses note that the bishop, as God's steward, must be free of these faults. Gill notes this shows that a bishop and an elder are the same (the Syriac renders it "an elder"); the character required is the same, with stress on his faithfulness as the steward of God's house. The witnesses note Paul requiring that a bishop be blameless as God's steward — not selfwilled, not soon angry, not given to wine, no striker, not greedy of filthy lucre; the bishop a steward who must be free from the vices that disqualify — that Paul, identifying the elder with the "bishop" (overseer), grounds the demand for blamelessness in his office: he is "the steward of God" (entrusted to manage God's household), and so must be free of a list of disqualifying faults — "not selfwilled" (not arrogant and self-pleasing), "not soon angry" (not quick-tempered), "not given to wine" (no drunkard), "no striker" (not violent or quarrelsome), "not given to filthy lucre" (not greedy for base gain) — so that the man who manages God's house must himself be free of these blemishes; not selfwilled, not soon angry, not given to wine, no striker. The witnesses note for a bishop must be blameless, as the steward of God. The witnesses note not selfwilled, not soon angry, not given to wine, no striker, not given to filthy lucre.

The witnesses note the bishop as God's steward must be blameless — not selfwilled, not soon angry, not given to wine, no striker, not greedy; free of the vices that disqualify.

So the witnesses make the verse the bishop a steward free of disqualifying vices. The lesson the expositors draw is that the overseer, entrusted with God's household, must be free of arrogance, temper, drink, violence, and greed. The witnesses note the steward: "for a bishop must be blameless, as the steward of God" — Gill notes the bishop is God's steward, managing his house; the steward; the of God; the the bishop; the manage; the entrusted. The expositors observe the bishop and elder same: Gill notes bishop and elder are the same; the bishop and elder; the the same; the overseer; the elder; the identified. The witnesses press the not selfwilled: "not selfwilled, not soon angry" — not arrogant, not quick-tempered; the not selfwilled; the not arrogant; the not soon angry; the not quick-tempered; the meek. The expositors draw the lesson on the not given to wine: "not given to wine, no striker" — no drunkard, not violent; the not given to wine; the no drunkard; the no striker; the not violent; the not quarrelsome. The witnesses observe the not filthy lucre: "not given to filthy lucre" — not greedy for base gain; the not filthy lucre; the not greedy; the base gain; the not covetous; the clean. The expositors note the free of blemishes: the man who manages God's house must be free of these blemishes; the free of blemishes; the God's house; the manage; the the steward; the clean. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for a bishop must be blameless as the steward of God, the not selfwilled not soon angry not given to wine no striker not given to filthy lucre, and the steward free of vices. The verse thus records the bishop a steward free of disqualifying vices — teaching that the overseer must be free of arrogance, temper, drink, violence, and greed.
8:
"But a lover of hospitality, a lover of good men, sober, just, holy, temperate;" The witnesses note the positive virtues required of the bishop. Barnes notes "a lover of good" (philagathos) may apply to good men or to any good thing; there is none more essential to a minister. The witnesses note Paul requiring that the bishop be a lover of hospitality, a lover of good, sober, just, holy, and temperate; the positive graces that must adorn the overseer — that Paul, having listed the faults the bishop must lack, names the virtues he must possess: "a lover of hospitality" (gladly welcoming and entertaining strangers and saints), "a lover of good men" (or of good in every form, loving what is good), "sober" (self-controlled and discreet), "just" (righteous in dealings with men), "holy" (devout toward God), and "temperate" (master of his appetites and passions) — so that the overseer is to be adorned with the whole circle of graces, godward, manward, and self-ward; a lover of hospitality, a lover of good men, sober, just, holy, temperate. The witnesses note a lover of hospitality, a lover of good men. The witnesses note sober, just, holy, temperate.

The witnesses note the bishop's positive virtues — a lover of hospitality and of good, sober, just, holy, temperate; the whole circle of graces godward, manward, and self-ward.

So the witnesses make the verse the positive graces of the overseer. The lesson the expositors draw is that the bishop must possess the whole circle of virtues toward God, men, and himself. The witnesses note the hospitality: "a lover of hospitality" — gladly welcoming strangers and saints; the hospitality; the welcoming; the strangers; the saints; the entertain. The expositors observe the lover of good: "a lover of good men" — Barnes notes a lover of good, in every form; the lover of good; the good men; the good; the every form; the loving good. The witnesses press the sober and just: "sober, just" — self-controlled and righteous in dealings; the sober; the just; the self-controlled; the righteous; the discreet. The expositors draw the lesson on the holy: "holy" — devout toward God; the holy; the devout; the toward God; the godward; the pious. The witnesses observe the temperate: "temperate" — master of his appetites; the temperate; the self-mastery; the appetites; the passions; the controlled. The expositors note the circle of graces: the overseer is adorned with the whole circle of graces; the circle of graces; the godward; the manward; the self-ward; the adorned. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the a lover of hospitality a lover of good men, the sober just holy temperate, and the positive graces. The verse thus records the positive graces of the overseer — teaching that the bishop must possess the whole circle of virtues toward God, men, and himself.
9:
"Holding fast the faithful word as he hath been taught, that he may be able by sound doctrine both to exhort and to convince the gainsayers." The witnesses note the elder's duty to hold the faithful word and refute error. Gill notes the "faithful word" is the doctrine of the gospel, so called because it is true and to be believed, the word of truth and truth itself, pure and unadulterated, which never deceived any who credited it. The witnesses note Paul requiring that the elder hold fast the faithful word, that by sound doctrine he may both exhort and convince the gainsayers; the doctrinal soundness that equips the elder to teach and refute — that Paul crowns the qualifications with the elder's grasp of truth: he must be "holding fast the faithful word as he hath been taught" (firmly retaining the sound apostolic doctrine), and that to a double end — "that he may be able by sound doctrine both to exhort" (to encourage and build up the faithful with wholesome teaching) "and to convince the gainsayers" (to refute and silence those who contradict the truth) — so that the elder is to be mighty in the Scriptures, both to feed the flock and to stop the mouths of error; holding fast the faithful word... both to exhort and to convince the gainsayers. The witnesses note holding fast the faithful word as he hath been taught. The witnesses note that he may be able by sound doctrine both to exhort and to convince the gainsayers.

The witnesses note the elder holding fast the faithful word — able by sound doctrine both to exhort the faithful and convince the gainsayers; mighty in Scripture to feed and refute.

So the witnesses make the verse the doctrinal soundness that equips the elder. The lesson the expositors draw is that the elder must hold the sound word fast, to both exhort the faithful and refute error. The witnesses note the holding fast: "holding fast the faithful word as he hath been taught" — Gill notes firmly retaining the sound apostolic doctrine; the holding fast; the the faithful word; the retaining; the the doctrine; the sound. The expositors observe the faithful word: the faithful word is the true, pure gospel; the the faithful word; the true; the pure; the the gospel; the unadulterated. The witnesses press the exhort: "both to exhort" — to encourage and build up the faithful; the exhort; the encourage; the build up; the the faithful; the wholesome. The expositors draw the lesson on the convince: "and to convince the gainsayers" — to refute and silence those who contradict; the convince; the refute; the silence; the gainsayers; the contradict. The witnesses observe the mighty in Scripture: the elder is to be mighty in the Scriptures; the mighty in Scripture; the the Scriptures; the strong; the able; the equipped. The expositors note the feed and stop: both to feed the flock and stop the mouths of error; the feed and stop; the feed; the the flock; the stop the mouths; the error. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the holding fast the faithful word as he hath been taught, the both to exhort and to convince the gainsayers, and the doctrinal soundness. The verse thus records the doctrinal soundness that equips the elder — teaching that the elder must hold the sound word fast, to both exhort the faithful and refute error.
10:
"For there are many unruly and vain talkers and deceivers, specially they of the circumcision:" The witnesses note the many false teachers, chiefly the Judaizers. Gill notes there were many unruly persons, who would not submit to the law of God or the gospel of Christ, nor to those set over them, nor be brought into regularity and order. The witnesses note Paul declaring that there are many unruly, vain talkers, and deceivers, especially those of the circumcision; the reason sound elders are needed — the many deceivers abroad — that Paul gives the urgent reason why the elder must be able to convince gainsayers (v.9): "for there are many" such adversaries — "unruly" (insubordinate, refusing all order and authority), "vain talkers" (full of empty, idle words) and "deceivers" (leading souls astray) — and "specially they of the circumcision," the Judaizing teachers who pressed the law and Jewish observances on the churches — so that the abundance and danger of these errorists makes sound, capable elders the more necessary; many unruly and vain talkers and deceivers, specially they of the circumcision. The witnesses note for there are many unruly and vain talkers and deceivers. The witnesses note specially they of the circumcision.

The witnesses note the many false teachers — unruly, vain talkers, and deceivers, chiefly the Judaizers — whose abundance makes sound, capable elders the more necessary.

So the witnesses make the verse the many deceivers needing refutation. The lesson the expositors draw is that the abundance of unruly deceivers, chiefly the Judaizers, makes sound elders necessary. The witnesses note the many: "for there are many unruly" — Gill notes many insubordinate, refusing all order; the many; the unruly; the insubordinate; the refusing order; the rebellious. The expositors observe the vain talkers: "and vain talkers and deceivers" — full of empty words, leading souls astray; the vain talkers; the empty words; the deceivers; the astray; the idle. The witnesses press the circumcision: "specially they of the circumcision" — chiefly the Judaizing teachers; the circumcision; the Judaizers; the the law; the Jewish; the pressing. The expositors draw the lesson on the the law pressed: the Judaizers pressed the law and Jewish observances; the the law pressed; the observances; the Jewish; the the churches; the urged. The witnesses observe the the danger: the abundance and danger of these errorists; the the danger; the abundance; the errorists; the many; the deceivers. The expositors note the elders needed: this makes sound, capable elders the more necessary; the elders needed; the sound; the capable; the necessary; the refute. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for there are many unruly and vain talkers and deceivers, the specially they of the circumcision, and the many deceivers. The verse thus records the many deceivers needing refutation — teaching that the abundance of unruly deceivers, chiefly the Judaizers, makes sound elders necessary.
11:
"Whose mouths must be stopped, who subvert whole houses, teaching things which they ought not, for filthy lucre's sake." The witnesses note that the deceivers must be silenced, for they overthrow households for gain. Gill notes their mouths must be stopped, that is, they must be silenced by reasons and arguments fetched from the word of God — as Christ silenced the Sadducees and Pharisees, and as Saul confounded the Jews at Damascus. The witnesses note Paul charging that the deceivers' mouths be stopped, since they subvert whole houses, teaching what they ought not for filthy lucre's sake; the silencing of greedy deceivers who ruin households — that Paul shows what is to be done with these errorists and why: their "mouths must be stopped" (silenced by sound argument from the Scripture, refuted and put to shame), because "they subvert whole houses" (overturn the faith of entire families), "teaching things which they ought not" (spreading doctrines they have no right to teach), and that "for filthy lucre's sake" (from a base motive of greed) — so that, being both destructive and mercenary, these teachers must be effectually refuted and stopped; whose mouths must be stopped, who subvert whole houses. The witnesses note whose mouths must be stopped. The witnesses note who subvert whole houses, teaching things which they ought not, for filthy lucre's sake.

The witnesses note the silencing of greedy deceivers — who subvert whole houses, teaching what they ought not for filthy lucre; destructive and mercenary, they must be refuted.

So the witnesses make the verse the silencing of greedy deceivers. The lesson the expositors draw is that the errorists, who ruin households for gain, must be refuted and stopped by the Scripture. The witnesses note the mouths stopped: "whose mouths must be stopped" — Gill notes silenced by argument from the word; the mouths stopped; the silenced; the by argument; the the word; the refuted. The expositors observe the subvert houses: "who subvert whole houses" — overturn the faith of entire families; the subvert houses; the overturn; the entire families; the the faith; the ruined. The witnesses press the teaching amiss: "teaching things which they ought not" — spreading doctrines they have no right to teach; the teaching amiss; the ought not; the no right; the doctrines; the spread. The expositors draw the lesson on the filthy lucre: "for filthy lucre's sake" — from a base motive of greed; the filthy lucre; the greed; the base motive; the gain; the mercenary. The witnesses observe the destructive and mercenary: being both destructive and mercenary, they must be stopped; the destructive and mercenary; the destructive; the mercenary; the stopped; the refuted. The expositors note the by the Scripture: they must be effectually refuted by the Scripture; the by the Scripture; the refuted; the effectually; the the word; the silenced. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the whose mouths must be stopped, the who subvert whole houses teaching things which they ought not for filthy lucre's sake, and the silencing of deceivers. The verse thus records the silencing of greedy deceivers — teaching that the errorists who ruin households for gain must be refuted and stopped.
12:
"One of themselves, even a prophet of their own, said, The Cretians are alway liars, evil beasts, slow bellies." The witnesses note Paul's citation of a Cretan poet against the Cretans. Chrysostom notes several questions arise here — who said this, why Paul quoted it, and why he brings forward a testimony from a heathen poet; Paul, as when he reasoned with the Athenians and cited their own poets, here turns the Cretans' own witness against them. The witnesses note Paul quoting a Cretan prophet of their own — that the Cretans are always liars, evil beasts, and lazy gluttons; the Cretans' own poet witnessing to their bad character — that Paul, to justify the sharp dealing the Cretan churches required, cites the testimony of "one of themselves, even a prophet of their own" (the Cretan poet Epimenides, esteemed among them as a seer), who had branded his own countrymen: "The Cretians are alway liars, evil beasts, slow bellies" — habitual liars, fierce and brutish in manners, and lazy, gluttonous idlers; the witnesses note Paul's wisdom in citing a pagan poet's own word, turning the nation's reputation, confirmed by their own authority, to show why a sharp rebuke was needed; the Cretians are alway liars, evil beasts, slow bellies. The witnesses note a prophet of their own. The witnesses note the Cretians are alway liars, evil beasts, slow bellies.

The witnesses note Paul citing the Cretans' own poet — "always liars, evil beasts, slow bellies" — turning the nation's reputation, confirmed by their own authority, to justify sharp rebuke.

So the witnesses make the verse the Cretans' own poet witnessing against them. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul cites a pagan poet's own testimony to confirm the Cretans' bad character and justify a sharp rebuke. The witnesses note the prophet: "a prophet of their own" — the Cretan poet Epimenides, esteemed as a seer; the prophet; the of their own; the Epimenides; the the poet; the the seer. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes Paul, as with the Athenians, cites their own poets; the Chrysostom; the the Athenians; the their own poets; the cited; the turned. The witnesses press the liars: "The Cretians are alway liars" — habitual liars; the liars; the always; the habitual; the deceit; the false. The expositors draw the lesson on the evil beasts: "evil beasts" — fierce and brutish in manners; the evil beasts; the fierce; the brutish; the manners; the savage. The witnesses observe the slow bellies: "slow bellies" — lazy, gluttonous idlers; the slow bellies; the lazy; the gluttonous; the idlers; the sluggish. The expositors note the their own authority: their reputation is confirmed by their own authority; the their own authority; the confirmed; the the nation; the reputation; the witness. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the a prophet of their own, the the Cretians are alway liars evil beasts slow bellies, and the Cretans' own witness. The verse thus records the Cretans' own poet witnessing against them — teaching that Paul cites a pagan poet's testimony to confirm their bad character and justify a sharp rebuke.
13:
"This witness is true. Wherefore rebuke them sharply, that they may be sound in the faith;" The witnesses note Paul's confirming of the poet's word and the charge to sharp rebuke. Gill notes Paul confirms what the poet had said, knowing it to be fact from his own experience and observation in the island; he does not say all the poet said was true, but this character of the Cretans was. The witnesses note Paul affirming that this witness is true, and charging that they be rebuked sharply, that they may be sound in the faith; the sharp rebuke aimed at making the erring sound — that Paul, endorsing the poet's verdict ("this witness is true," confirmed by his own observation of the Cretans), draws the practical charge: "wherefore rebuke them sharply" — deal with such hardened, faulty characters not gently but with cutting severity, as their case requires — and that with a healing aim, "that they may be sound in the faith"; so that the sharpness is no cruelty but a surgeon's, meant to cure the disease of error and bring them to soundness; rebuke them sharply, that they may be sound in the faith. The witnesses note this witness is true. The witnesses note rebuke them sharply, that they may be sound in the faith.

The witnesses note Paul confirming the poet's word and charging sharp rebuke — not gently but with cutting severity, with the healing aim of making the erring sound in the faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the sharp rebuke aimed at soundness. The lesson the expositors draw is that the hardened Cretans need cutting rebuke, but with the healing aim of making them sound in the faith. The witnesses note the witness true: "this witness is true" — Gill notes Paul confirms the poet's character of the Cretans; the witness true; the confirmed; the the poet; the his observation; the the character. The expositors observe the not all true: Paul does not say all the poet said was true, but this; the not all true; the not all; the but this; the the character; the confirmed. The witnesses press the rebuke sharply: "wherefore rebuke them sharply" — deal with cutting severity; the rebuke sharply; the cutting; the severity; the not gently; the sharp. The expositors draw the lesson on the their case: as their hardened, faulty case requires; the their case; the hardened; the faulty; the requires; the severe. The witnesses observe the sound in the faith: "that they may be sound in the faith" — the healing aim of soundness; the sound in the faith; the healing aim; the soundness; the cure; the the aim. The expositors note the surgeon's: the sharpness is a surgeon's, meant to cure; the surgeon's; the cure; the the disease; the error; the heal. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the this witness is true, the rebuke them sharply that they may be sound in the faith, and the sharp rebuke for soundness. The verse thus records the sharp rebuke aimed at soundness — teaching that the hardened Cretans need cutting rebuke, but with the healing aim of making them sound in the faith.
14:
"Not giving heed to Jewish fables, and commandments of men, that turn from the truth." The witnesses note the charge to turn from Jewish fables and the commandments of men. Gill notes the Jewish fables concerned God, the angels, the creation, the giving of the law, the Messiah and his earthly kingdom, and the feast for the righteous — idle traditions of the elders. The witnesses note Paul charging that they give no heed to Jewish fables and the commandments of men that turn from the truth; the rejection of Jewish myth and man-made rule — that Paul names what the Cretan believers, made sound in the faith, must shun: "Jewish fables" (the idle myths and traditions of the rabbis) "and commandments of men" (human rules and impositions, not God's) — both of which "turn from the truth" (lead the soul away from the true gospel) — so that soundness in the faith means refusing both the fanciful legends and the man-made commandments by which the Judaizers drew men from Christ; not giving heed to Jewish fables, and commandments of men. The witnesses note not giving heed to Jewish fables. The witnesses note commandments of men, that turn from the truth.

The witnesses note the charge to shun Jewish fables and the commandments of men — both of which turn the soul from the truth; soundness means refusing legend and man-made rule.

So the witnesses make the verse the rejection of Jewish myth and man-made rule. The lesson the expositors draw is that soundness in the faith means refusing both Jewish fables and human commandments that lead from the truth. The witnesses note the Jewish fables: "not giving heed to Jewish fables" — Gill notes the idle myths and traditions of the rabbis; the Jewish fables; the the myths; the the traditions; the the rabbis; the idle. The expositors observe the the content: the fables concerned God, angels, creation, the Messiah's earthly kingdom; the the content; the God; the angels; the the Messiah; the legends. The witnesses press the commandments of men: "and commandments of men" — human rules and impositions, not God's; the commandments of men; the human rules; the impositions; the not God's; the man-made. The expositors draw the lesson on the turn from truth: "that turn from the truth" — both lead the soul away from the true gospel; the turn from truth; the lead away; the the gospel; the the truth; the astray. The witnesses observe the soundness means refusing: soundness means refusing both legend and rule; the soundness means refusing; the the legends; the the rules; the refuse; the shun. The expositors note the Judaizers drew men: by these the Judaizers drew men from Christ; the the Judaizers; the drew men; the from Christ; the away; the seduced. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the not giving heed to Jewish fables, the commandments of men that turn from the truth, and the rejection of myth and rule. The verse thus records the rejection of Jewish myth and man-made rule — teaching that soundness in the faith means refusing both Jewish fables and human commandments that lead from the truth.
15:
"Unto the pure all things are pure: but unto them that are defiled and unbelieving is nothing pure; but even their mind and conscience is defiled." The witnesses note the principle that purity is in the heart, not in things. Gill notes the apostle, having mentioned Jewish fables and traditions, takes notice of the notion these Judaizing Christians had retained — that some things, touched, handled, or tasted, occasioned uncleanness; against this he sets the truth. The witnesses note Paul declaring that to the pure all things are pure, but to the defiled and unbelieving nothing is pure, their very mind and conscience being defiled; the seat of purity and pollution in the heart, not in outward things — that Paul overthrows the Judaizers' ceremonial scruples with a great principle: "unto the pure all things are pure" (to the man whose heart is cleansed by faith, all God's creatures, foods and the like, are clean and lawful to use) — but "unto them that are defiled and unbelieving is nothing pure," for the defilement is not in the thing but in the man; their unbelief and inward corruption taint everything they touch, since "even their mind and conscience is defiled" — so that purity and pollution lie not in meats or ceremonies but in the heart; unto the pure all things are pure. The witnesses note unto the pure all things are pure. The witnesses note unto them that are defiled and unbelieving is nothing pure; but even their mind and conscience is defiled.

The witnesses note that purity lies in the heart, not in things — to the pure all things are pure, but to the defiled nothing is pure, their very mind and conscience corrupted.

So the witnesses make the verse the seat of purity in the heart. The lesson the expositors draw is that purity and pollution lie not in outward things but in the cleansed or defiled heart. The witnesses note the pure all pure: "unto the pure all things are pure" — Gill notes to the heart cleansed by faith, all things are clean; the pure all pure; the cleansed; the by faith; the all things; the clean. The expositors observe the the Judaizers: against the Judaizers' ceremonial scruples about meats; the the Judaizers; the ceremonial; the scruples; the meats; the touched. The witnesses press the nothing pure: "but unto them that are defiled and unbelieving is nothing pure" — to the defiled nothing is pure; the nothing pure; the the defiled; the unbelieving; the tainted; the polluted. The expositors draw the lesson on the in the man: the defilement is not in the thing but in the man; the in the man; the not the thing; the the defilement; the the heart; the polluted. The witnesses observe the mind and conscience: "but even their mind and conscience is defiled" — their very mind and conscience are corrupted; the mind and conscience; the defiled; the corrupted; the inward; the tainted. The expositors note the not in meats: purity lies not in meats or ceremonies but the heart; the not in meats; the not ceremonies; the the heart; the the seat; the purity. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the unto the pure all things are pure, the unto them that are defiled and unbelieving is nothing pure but even their mind and conscience is defiled, and the seat of purity. The verse thus records the seat of purity in the heart — teaching that purity and pollution lie not in outward things but in the cleansed or defiled heart.
16:
"They profess that they know God; but in works they deny him, being abominable, and disobedient, and unto every good work reprobate." The witnesses note the hypocrisy of those who profess God but deny him in deeds. Gill notes these persons professed to know God — that there is a God, one only true God, the God of Israel — yet this knowledge was but notional, for they were judaizing Christians whose profession was contradicted by their lives. The witnesses note Paul declaring that they profess to know God but in works deny him, being abominable, disobedient, and unto every good work reprobate; the empty profession contradicted by an ungodly life — that Paul exposes the deepest fault of these defiled teachers: their religion is all in word, none in deed — "they profess that they know God" (claiming the knowledge of the true God) "but in works they deny him" (their conduct flatly contradicting their creed) — and he brands their character: "abominable" (detestable in God's sight), "disobedient" (rebellious against his will), and "unto every good work reprobate" (worthless and disqualified for any good deed) — so that a profession of God belied by an ungodly life is the very mark of these hypocrites; in works they deny him, being abominable, and disobedient. The witnesses note they profess that they know God; but in works they deny him. The witnesses note being abominable, and disobedient, and unto every good work reprobate.

The witnesses note the empty profession contradicted by deeds — professing to know God but denying him in works, abominable, disobedient, reprobate to every good work.

So the witnesses make the verse the profession belied by an ungodly life. The lesson the expositors draw is that a claimed knowledge of God contradicted by wicked works is the mark of these hypocrites. The witnesses note the profess: "they profess that they know God" — Gill notes they claimed to know the true God; the profess; the know God; the claimed; the notional; the the knowledge. The expositors observe the in works deny: "but in works they deny him" — their conduct contradicts their creed; the in works deny; the contradicts; the their conduct; the their creed; the deny. The witnesses press the abominable: "being abominable" — detestable in God's sight; the abominable; the detestable; the God's sight; the loathsome; the vile. The expositors draw the lesson on the disobedient: "and disobedient" — rebellious against his will; the disobedient; the rebellious; the his will; the against; the unruly. The witnesses observe the reprobate: "and unto every good work reprobate" — worthless and disqualified for any good deed; the reprobate; the worthless; the disqualified; the every good work; the unfit. The expositors note the profession belied: a profession belied by an ungodly life is the mark of hypocrites; the profession belied; the ungodly life; the the mark; the hypocrites; the contradicted. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the they profess that they know God but in works they deny him, the being abominable and disobedient and unto every good work reprobate, and the empty profession. The verse thus records the profession belied by an ungodly life — teaching that a claimed knowledge of God contradicted by wicked works is the mark of these hypocrites.
